statement.markdown: Given an array of integers, determine whether it's possible to delete any subarray within the array such that the resulting array has all bits set to 0. The subarray to be deleted can be chosen from any subset of the array and can be non-contiguous.
statement.inputFormat: Input will consist of an array of integers.
statement.outputFormat: Output a boolean value, True if deletion of a subarray is possible and False otherwise.

examples.explanation: No subarray can be removed to make array [0, 0, 0, 0]

hints.text: Use bitwise operations to solve this problem.

editorial.title: Bitwise Deletion Editorial
editorial.intuition: Consider using bitmask and binary operations.
editorial.bruteForce: Naively iterate through the array and remove elements if they are not 0.
editorial.betterSolution: Use bitwise operations like AND and XOR to track the current bits in the array.
editorial.optimalSolution: Iterate through the array and use bitwise operations to determine if a subarray can be removed.

starterCode.cpp: class Solution { public: bool solve(vector<int>& A); };
starterCode.java: public class Solution { public boolean solve(int[] A); }
starterCode.python: class Solution: def solve(self, A: list[int]): bool:

buggyStarterCode.cpp: class Solution { public: bool solve(vector<int>& A) { int bitmask = 0; for (int x : A) bitmask ^= x; return (bitmask == 0) && (A.size() == 0); } };
buggyStarterCode.java: public class Solution { public boolean solve(int[] A) { int bitmask = 0; for (int x : A) bitmask ^= x; return (bitmask == 0) && (A.length == 0); } }
buggyStarterCode.python: class Solution: def solve(self, A: list[int]) -> bool: bitmask = 0; for x in A: bitmask ^= x; return (bitmask == 0) and (not A)
buggyStarterCode.javascript: function solve(A) { let bitmask = 0; for (let x of A) bitmask ^= x; return (bitmask == 0) && (A.length == 0); }

solutions.cpp:
class Solution { public: bool solve(vector<int>& A) { int bitmask = 0; for (int x : A) bitmask ^= x; return bitmask == 0; } };
int main() { vector<int> A = { 2, 5, 7, 1 }; Solution s; cout << (s.solve(A) ? 'T' : 'F'); return 0; }
solutions.java:
public class Solution { public boolean solve(int[] A) { int bitmask = 0; for (int x : A) bitmask ^= x; return bitmask == 0; } }
public class Main { public static void main(String[] args) { int[] A = { 2, 5, 7, 1 }; Solution s = new Solution(); System.out.println(s.solve(A) ? 'T' : 'F'); } }
